inscription.head:
No. 224.
(A.R. No. 224 of 1908.)
KŌVILŪR, PATTUKKOTTAI TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: Inner gōpura of the Mantrapurīśvara temple.

This inscription may be assigned on palaeographical considerations to
c. 13th century.

It states that a daṇḍanāyaka (name not known) granted the lands
as iṟaiyili and confirmed that the worship and services are to be continued
to the deity Uḍaiyār Tiruvuśāttāṉam-uḍaiya-ṉāyaṉār. Further it is known
that a stipulation has been made to the effect that capital in terms of paddy
should not be received (collected) from the dēvadāṉa lands granted as iṟaiyili
from the eighteenth year. The imprecatory portion is somewhat interesting
in this record.

inscription.head:
No. 225.
(A.R. No. 225 of 1908.)
KŌVILŪR, PATTUKKOTTAI TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: Outer gōpura of the Mantrapurīśvara temple.

This epigraph may be assigned to c. 13th century on palaeographical
considerations.

It seems to register some gift by the communities like vaṇṇār
(washerman), nāvidar (barber), paḷḷar, paṟaiyar, etc to provide for the
services to the deity Uśāttāṉam-uḍaiyār. The order (ōlai) issued to this effect
on the fourth year of his reign was attested by one of his officers named
[Muṉaiyada]raiyan. The writing at the end of lines in the right of the
inscription has been erased by chiselling. Hence, the details of the royal
order are not clear.

edition.l: muṟ uṭaiyār tiru[ ucāttā-
ṉa] muṭaiya[ṉāyaṉār] tiruma-

inscription.head:
No. 226.
(A.R. No. 226 of 1908.)
KŌVILŪR, PATTUKKOTTAI TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the outer gōpura of the Mantrapurīśvara temple.

This damaged inscription may be assigned to c. 14th century on
palaeographical considerations.

It seems to record the grant of land probably as iṟaiyili by the ūravar
of Periyakōṭṭai and Kāḍandai-kuḍi to the temple of the deity
Tiruvuśāttāṉam-[uḍaiyār]. This document was attested by several signatories
including Tiru[mala]pāḍi-uḍaiyār and others.

inscription.head:
No. 227.
(A.R. No. 227 of 1908.)
KŌVILŪR, PATTUKKOTTAI TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: On the outer gōpura of the Mantrapurīśvara temple.

This damaged epigraph may be assigned to c. 13th century on
palaeographical grounds.
It seems to record an order (ōlai) issued by a certain [Śō]ḻiyavaraiyaṉ.

It seems to record some grant of land made to the temple of
Tiruvuśāttāṉam-uḍaiyār. This order was attested by the [ūravar] of
Koṅgaṇakkuḍi and the kāraṇavar of Kāḍandaikkuḍi. Besides these, a
number of others such as the nāṭṭavar of Ālattūr, are also referred to have
signed this document. Since some of the signatories of this order figure
in a record dated in the 13th+1st year of the reign of Chaḍaiyavarmaṉ
Sundara-pāṇḍya, the present inscription may also be assigned to the king
mentioned above (A.R.Ep., 1908, No. 226).

l.ta: ṉāṭṭavar eḻuttu ivai ma ••••• nāṭṭavar eḻuttu

inscription.head:
No. 228.
(A.R. No. 228 of 1908.)
KŌDAVALI, ••••• TALUK, GODAVARI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: Rock-cut well on the hill (north wall)

This Prākrit inscription in Brāhmī characters of the third century A.D.,
furnishes the details of date viz., year 2, ma 1, he, pa 2, di 1. These details
correspond to December 210 A.D.

It refers to the reign of Chaṁḍasāti and records the establishment of
the gift (dhama) of a khaṁgu (rock-cut well ?) by the minister Sasa of
Khaḍḍavali.
Published in Epigraphia Indica, Vol. XVIII, pp. 318. ff.

edition.l: caṁḍasātisa [ra]ji vachare 2
ma | he pa 2 di | khaḍḍa[va]li

inscription.head:
No. 229.
(A.R. No. 229 of 1908.)
BĀLŌD, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: On a stone set up on the road leading to the river.

The record in late Brāhmī characters may be assigned on
palaeographical grounds to c. third century A.D.

This fragmentary inscription mentions a certain Sanakasa. Other details
are not clear.

inscription.head:
No. 230.
(A.R. No. 230 of 1908.)
BĀLŌD, DRUG DISTRICT, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: Stone found on the road side is now preserved in the Nagpur Museum.

This worn out Nāgarī record is illegible mostly but for the date portion
Saṁvat 1005. It seems to refer to some brāhmaṇas, Rālaṇa, etc. Other details
are not clear.
On two pillars set up in front of Dantēśvara temple.

This damaged inscription furnishes the details of date viz., Śaka 982,
Sārvari, Kārthika śu 5, Monday corresponding to 1060 A.D., October 2.

The details referring to the [Telugu]-Chōḷa chief Chandrāditya and his
pious acts are identical with those mentioned in the sequel in an inscription
of this king. (A.R.Ep., 1908, No. 334; S.I.I., Vol. X, No. 645.). The portion
mentioning the names of witnesses to the grant is damaged (Text lines 90-
105). Only the names of Malli-seṭṭi and Daṇḍīśvara-naṁbi could be traced
from the extant portion. The name Kētāchāri seems to be the engraver
of this record.
Published in South Indian Inscriptions, Vol. X, No. 644.

edition.l: damaged.
damaged.
damaged.

inscription.head:
No. 232.
(A.R. No. 232 of 1908.)
BARSŪR, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: Stone in a ruined temple, west of the village.

This Nāgarī record may be assigned on palaeographical considerations
to c. 14th century.

This seems to record the construction of a temple (kōṭi) probably to
the deity Chāmuṇḍadēvī. Other details are not clear.

l.note: The letter ‘u’ is written below ‘da’ in both the places.
The letter ‘u’ is written below ‘da’ in both the places.

inscription.head:
No. 233.
(A.R. No. 233 of 1908.)
BARSŪR, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: Stone lying in the varandah of the school.

There are three slabs, one of which contains the names of some
goddesses such as Mahishāsuramardini.

Another is a fragmentary record which mentions [Ka]nnaradēva. The
third record refers to the date Śaka 1242 (1320 A.D.) and mentions a few
names ending with dēva.

inscription.head:
No. 234.
(A.R. No. 234 of 1908.)
BHAIRAMGARH, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: Slab setup near the police station, being brought from Jaṅgla-Pōtēnār.

This epigraph furnishes the details of date viz., Śaka 982, Sārvari,
Kārttika śu 5, Monday corresponding to 1060 A.D., October 2.

It states that the king was camping at Bārasūru. A feudatory of his
was the mahāmaṇḍalēśvara Chaṁdrādityarāju, who is stated to have
belonged to the Solar race (dinakara-kula), to the Kāśyapa-gōtra and to the
Karikāla family. It also mentions that this chief was the lord of Kāvvāri
(i.e., Kāvēri). that his crest was a lion, that he was the lord of Oṟayūra
(Uṟaiyūr), that he was born of the Chōḷa-vaṁśa and that he was the chief
of the village Ammagāma. The portion mentioning the titles, etc and the
grant details are the same as the ones found in another record of the same
king. (No. 231).

The introductory portion referring to the Chōḍa chief Chandrāditya
is identical with that of the Telugu-Chōḍa chiefs of the Cuddapah region.
Both appear to have had their origin from a common stock and thus claims
to have belonged to the family of Karikāla (Karikāl-ānvaya).

It states that the chief Chandrāditya had built a tank called
Chandrāditya-samudra at his capital Bārasūra (Barsūr) and on its bank
constructed a temple to Śiva whom he had set up there in the name of
Chandrādityēśvara. He is stated to have granted lands in Vaḍḍa-nāṇḍu as
dēvabhōga to this temple with the permission of Dhārāvarusa (i.e.,
Dhārāvarsha).

This Dhārāvarusa is identical with the Sinda king Jagadēkabhūshaṇa-
mahārāja Dhārāvarsha.

The sthānapati of this temple Dharmarāśi is stated to have established
a flower-garden, named after the chief Chandrāditya.

The witnesses to the endowment are Budda-nāyakuṇḍu, Suṁkaya-
Dēma-nāyakuṇḍu, the rājaguru Rudrapaṇḍita, Komāra Nārāyaṇaśeṭṭi,
Aniyaṁka Mallaśeṭṭi, and Aṁgaḍi Duggiśeṭṭi.
Published in South Indian Inscriptions, Vol. X, No. 645.

l.te: na rāju galgenēni vāraṇāsi vēya gavilala-

inscription.p: Slab set up near the police station, being brought from Jaṅgla-Pōtēnār.

This incomplete record may palaeographically be assigned to about the
12th Century A.D.

It states that the king belonged to the Nāgavaṁśa and to the Kāśyapa-
gōtra. He is mentioned as the lord of Bhōgavatī, whose crest is a tiger
with a calf and who is the worshipper of the feet of Māṇikyadēvī.

It is possible that the Nāgavaṁśi rulers worshipped Durgā under the
name of Māṇikyadēvī. (See Ep. Ind., Vol. X, p. 27).

It further mentions a son (name not given) of [mahā]maṇḍalēśvara
Raṁgāyadēva-mahārāju, whose identity is not known.
Published in South Indian Inscriptions, Vol. X, No. 649.

edition.l: ju lēta śrīmava¨rdhaShould be read as ‘śrīmanmahā’-

inscription.p: Slab set up near the police station, being removed from Jaṅgla-Pōtēnār.

This fragmentary inscription may palaeographically be assigned to c.
12th Century A.D. It seems to record some grant of land. Other details
are not known.

edition.h4:
TEXT. Contains the symbols of Sun and Moon at the top and a standing bull at the
bottom of the writing. This record is a fragment.

inscription.head:
No. 237.
(A.R. No. 237 of 1908.)
BHAIRAMGAḌH, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: Slab set up at the Police station.
The record may be assigned on palaeographical grounds to a late period.

It mentions Śrī Magaradhvaja 700, meaning an ascetic with his seven
hundred disciples who in all probability might have gone from one place
to another.

(see. Hiralal, Inscriptions in Central Province and Birar, p. 156 and p.
113).

inscription.head:
No. 238-241.
(A.R. No. 238 to 241 of 1908.)
CHAPKA, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: Stones set up on the bank of Mārkaṇḍēya river.

These sati stone records are in late Nāgarī characters except No. 239
which is in Oriya characters.

inscription.head:
No. 238.
(A.R. No. 238 of 1908.)
inscription.p: It reads •• rasanāma rā'ṅta.

inscription.head:
No. 241.
(A.R. No. 241 of 1908.)
inscription.p: Text not available.
Slab set up near the north prākāra of the temple of Dantēśvarī.
This inscription may palaeographically be assigned to c. 12th Century.

It refers to the reign of Rājabhūshaṇa, a king of the Chinda-kula. It
states that the king belonged to the Nāgavaṁśa. He had a tiger crest. It
records a grant made by Māsakadēvī. Also reference is made to
Chakragoṭṭamu, being occupied by rāchakoḍukulu (crown-princes or sons of
the king). It seems that the mention of different kāṁpulu, balaṁjalu,
vaḍugāra jāyalu, sommarivāru, nāḍigāṁ, jōrigāṁpulu, illagaṭṭina-kāṁpulu,
pulla-kāṁpulu, etc., in connection with the grant, indicate their presence as
witnesses to the grant.

We know from the Kuruspāl inscriptions of Dhāraṇa-mahādēvī that
Rājabhūshaṇa was an epithet of king Sōmēśvaradēva.

The reference to Chakragoṭṭamu as the residence of princes of the
Nāgavaṁśi family in this record is interesting.
Published in South Indian Inscriptions, Vol. X, No. 650.

inscription.p: Slab in the temple of Dantēśvarī.

This damaged record furnishes the details of date viz., Śaka 984,
Āshāḍha, Thursday which are not sufficient for verification.

It states that when the king was camping (nelevīḍu) at the place called
Dattavāḍa, he made a gift of thirty māḍas. The details of the grant, the
donee or the temple, etc., are not clear.
(Published in South Indian Inscriptions, Vol. X, No. 646).

edition.l: Illegible.
Illegible.
Illegible.

inscription.head:
No. 244.
(A.R. No. 244 of 1908.)
DANTĒWĀRĀ, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: Slab in the Dantēśvarī temple.

This Sanskrit inscription in Nāgarī script is dated in Saṁvat 1760,
Vaiśākha, vadi 3 (3rd day of dark fortnight).

It records the genealogy of this king commencing from Kākati
Pratāparudra, who was the king of Ōraṅgal (Warangal). Also the military
achievements of Dikpāladēva are recorded. It states that Annamarāja, the
brother of Pratāparudra was the first to come to Bastar. The genealogy
of nine successors upto the time of Dikpāladēva is narrated here.

Then, it records the tour undertaken by Rāja Dikpāladēva to the
Dantēśvarī temple, when ‘so many thousands of buffaloes and goats were
sacrificed, that the waters of Śaṅkhinī river became red like kusuma flowers
and remained so for five days’. The Kākatīyas are mentioned to be
Sōmavaṁśīs, born of the Pāṇḍava Arjuna. The tour to Daṁtāvalā is
mentioned as a kuṭuṁba-jātrā. It was intended probably to celebrate a festival
to propiate his tutelary deity evidently Dantēśvarī.

|| katī pratāparudranāmā rājā oraṁgaladeśe samabhavat || yasyedaṁ padyaṁ na-
|| paramagrahāra pīḍā kucakuṁbheṣu kuraṁgalocanānāṁ || tasyai kadā svarṇṇavṛ-

mahārājādhirājo dikpāladevadevo yathārtha nāmā śatavarṣāvadhi niṣkaṁṭakāṁ
mahīpālayati || tena caikadāśvapura[ni*]vāsi janena sahadaṁtāvalāṁ samāganya kuṭuṁ-
bajātrākṛta || tatra bahusahasramahiṣa chāgaśarīra saṁyāṁta stktapravāhaiḥ śaṁkhinīṁ-

nadīṁ śroṇitodāmakarot || ityarthaṁ likhitaṁ prastheti [satvā]caṁdratārakaṁ ||
dikpāla-

deva sadṛśo bhuṇena bhavitā kalau || 1 || samvat 1760 vaijhāśa vadi 3 yā
likhitaṁ śrī

inscription.head:
No. 245.
(A.R. No. 245 of 1908.)
DANTĒWĀRĀ, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: Pillar in the Dantēśvarī temple.

This damaged inscribed pillar was then being used as a yūpa (i.e.,
sacrificial post). This slab is inscribed on all the four faces and at the
top. It contains fifty lines of writing and a major portion is damaged.

The extant portion is in Telugu characters of 13th century. The details
of date given are Śaka 1147, Jyēshṭha ba 10. The date corresponds to 13th
June 1224 A.D. But if the year 1147 is taken as an expired one, then it
would correspond to 2nd June 1225 A.D.

It seems to record some gift to the temple of Dēvī on the above date.
It is not clear as to the donor of the grant.

(Published in Epigraphia Indica, Vol. X, pp. 40-42 and South Indian
Inscriptions, Vol. X, No. 648).

edition.l: masta praśastaRead ‘praśasti’-

inscription.head:
No. 246.
(A.R. No. 246 of 1908.)
DANTĒWĀRĀ, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: On a slab in the Dantēśvari temple.

This is a Maithilī rendering of No. 244 written by the rājaguru, of
Kākatīya family, who was a Maithila Paṇḍit.

This is dated in the Vikrama Saṁvat 1760. i.e., 1703 A.D. on the 3rd
day of Baisākh, dark fortnight. This records the pilgrimage of Rāja
Dikpāladēva to the Dantēśvari shrine when ‘so many thousands of buffaloes
and goats were sacrificed that the waters of the Saṅkhinī river became
red like kusuma flowers and remained so for five days.’

The Kākatīyas are stated to be Sōmavaṁśīs, born of the Pāṇḍava
Arjuna.
(Published in the Ep. Ind., Vol. IX., p. 164).

inscription.head:
No. 247.
(A.R. No. 247 of 1908.)
DOṄGAR, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
Dariāvadēva Saṁvat 1836 = 1779 A.D.
inscription.p: On a slab lying near the Dantēśvari temple.

This Hindi inscription in Nāgarī script is dated in Saṁvat 1836 i.e., 1779
A.D. This record refers to a visit of Rāja Dariāvadēva, born of Pāṇḍavārjuna
dynasty, in order to quell a local rebellion. It refers to a certain
mahāpradhāna (name not clear).

inscription.head:
No. 248.
(A.R. No. 248 of 1908.)
DŌṄGAR, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: On a slab lying nerar the Dantēśvari temple.

This Hindi inscription in Nāgarī script is dated in Saṁvat 1928, i.e.,
1871 A.D. records the paṭṭābhishēka ceremony of Bhairāmadēvas, belonging
to Pāṇḍavārjuna-kula, the father of the present Bastar chief.
Slab from Errakōṭa, now being preserved at Jagdālpūr.

This incomplete epigraph furnishes the details of date viz., Śaka 945,
Rudhirōdgāri, Vaiśākha śu 3, Thursday.

It refers to [Nṛipa*]ti-bhūshaṇa-mahāraju as belonging to Chinda-kula
and Nāgavaṁśa. Other details are lost. Just as Rājabhūshaṇa was a surname
of king Sōmēśvara I, [Nṛipa*]ti-bhūshaṇa was another surname for him.

inscription.head:
No. 250.
(A.R. No. 250 of 1908.)
GĀDIA, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: Slab lying in the jungle to the west of the village.

This inscription may palaeographically be assigned to c. 11th century.
The details of date Śaka 1019, Kārttika, trayōdaśi tithi are not sufficient
for verification.

The titles given in this record are found mentioned in the Kuruspāl
stone inscription of Sōmēśvaradēva of the same date (Ep. Ind., Vol. X, pp.
37-38) except the epithet Rājabhū[shaṇa*].

It records the gift of some lands (details are lost) by Kannarāju. The
details of the land grant, the purpose, etc., are lost since the record is damaged
very much. The witnesses to the grant are Bēti-seṭṭi, Malli-seṭṭi, Māni-seṭṭi
and others of Bhairavadurgga.

The title Rājabhūshaṇa is mentioned to have been attributed to king
Sōmēśvara, not only in his own records, but also in the two Kuruspāl records
of Dharaṇa-mahādēvī of the time of Sōmēśvaradēva.
(Ep. Ind., Vol. X, p. 32 and p. 34)

l.note: The rest of the lines are illegeble.

inscription.head:
No. 251.
(A.R. No. 251 of 1908.)
JATANPĀL, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: On a slab from this village (now at Jagadalpūr).

This record is in corrupt Sanskrit prose engraved in the Nāgarī
characters.

There are altogether eighteen lines but lines 16 and 17 are covered
with the imprecatory figures of a pig and an ass.

On the top of this epigraph there are figures of the sun and the moon
together with seven small circles in a row apparently representing the
remaining seven planets. The significance of these is that the grant is to
last as long as the navagrahas (the nine planets) endure, while the figures
at the bottom represent a curse on the transgressor of the gift declaring
his father, an ass and the mother, a pig.

It records the grant of land made by one Kāmā-nāyaka to one Rāhīla-
pāṁḍe. The mention of the mother Gaṁgādēvī, after the king's name
is not clear.

The record does not furnish any other details of date except the Śaka
year 1140 which corresponds to 1218 A.D. In this year there was an eclipse
of the Sun and the month of Jyēshṭha was intercalary. It is so significant
that it was doubly meritorious to make a gift.

Kāmā-nāyaka is mentioned as a chief (maṇḍalika) of Mahārāja
Narasiṁhadēva. This king is known to have held the title Jagadēkabhūshaṇa
as is evident from his Dantēwārā record (A.R.Ep., 1908, No. 245). He
apparently belonged to the Nāgavaṁśa dynasty.

The names of Mēdani Pāṁḍe, Dēva-nāyaka, Jāmu Sāhu, Ghīkā Sēṭhi
and Sōmāi are found mentioned as the protectors of this grant.
(Published in Epigraphia Indica, Vol. X, p. 42).

l.de: paye | svadata ca pradāca vā johato va-

inscription.head:
No. 252.
(A.R. No. 252 of 1908.)
GĀDIA, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p:
On a slab found in the ruins between the Dīwān and Śītālā tanks
in this town.

This Sanskrit record in Nāgarī characters is dated Śaka 1242, Raudra,
Jyēshṭha ba 5 corresponding to 1320 A.D., May 27.

It furnishes the genealogy of the Kākaira chiefs commencing from
Siṁharāj upto Bhānudēva. The genealogical order is as follows:
Siṁharāj
Vyāghra
Vōpadēva
Kṛishṇa
Jaitarāja
Sōmachandra
Bhānudēva

It also gives the genealogy of the minister of king Bhānudēva for four
generations. While tracing the eulogy of the nāyaka Vāsudēva, the minister
of king Bhānudēva, it records the construction of the two temples of
Mahādēva and another in honour of the local deity of the fields besides
a building with a gateway (pratōli) and the excavation of two tanks.

The expression kauḍika-bandha is interesting. Probably it refers to the
damming up of a tank (taḍāga). The place Kākaira is mentioned as the
capital of king Bhānudēva.

The genealogy of the minister Vāsudēva is given below:
Dāmōdara
Pōlū-nāyaka
Bhīma
Vāsudēva (resident of the town Kākaira)
The record was written by Śaktikumāra.
(Published in Ep. Ind., Vol. IX, Text pp. 126-27).

l.de:
oṁ sidviḥ || āśīdvaṁśe himāṁsormmahitaguṇagaṇa ssiṁha rādverisiṁhasta
smādyāghrāvanīśo bhava-

datula yaśāhā tejasāpāstaśūryaḥ || jase sopi sva vīryocita nṛpatilakaṁ
bopadevaṁ sa caivaṁ kṛṣṇā-

khyaṁ vairirājavrajadalanaparaṁ vikramākrāṁta viśvaṁ || 1 || kākaire vanipapāla
maulimukuṭaprodbhā-

sihīrāṁkura jyoti dyotitapādapaṁkaja nakajyoti mrakāśā bhuvi ||
saṁgrāmāṁgaṇa vīravikra-

maguṇaḥ śrījaitarājo bhavattasmādadbhuta satpratāpamahasaḥ śrīsomacaṁdronṛpaḥ
|| 2 || tasmātśrī-

jāgertti pratipakṣapakṣadalano bhūpāla cūḍārmāṇaḥ yasminśāśati loka eṣa
sukṛtī jāgartti śa-

dvāsanaḥ || 3 || deśaḥ puṇyanirīti iḍāsvata dhanassatkarmmaniṣṭhā dvijāḥ
sveṣṭhāpūrtta parāḥ prajāṣki-

maparaṁ paurāḥ paraṁ dhārmmikāḥ | samyāḥ śāstravicāra dhautamanaso
dhammavitāra kalau rājyaṁ śāsa-

ti bhānudevanṛpatau kiṁ kiṁ na lokottaraṁ || 4 || vaṁśe nāgada lopajīvana
janasphīte bhavannā-

yakaḥ śrīdāmodara sūnurujvalayaśā polūprajā nāyakaḥ || khyātastattanayaḥ
svabhāvasara-

lo bhīmābhidhastasuto kākaire nṛpabhānudeva nagare śrīvāsudevobhavat
|| 5 || staṁbhoyaṁ [na]-

sudavo bhuvi || 6 || devaśrīśasibhūṣaṇasya kṛtinā devālayaṁ kāritaṁ
yugmaṁ maṁḍapaśobhitaṁ ca

puratobhadraṁ pratolyā saha | kṣetresasya tathā surālayavaraṁ sphītaṁ [ta]
ḍāgaṁ tathā baṁdhaṁ kauḍikasaṁjñakaṁ

vasya visphārā sthitācandrārkatārakaṁ || 8 || saṁvat 1242 raudra
saṁvatsare jyeṣṭa vadi-

paṁcamyāṁ | prasastissamāropitā nāyakavāsudevena likhitā saktikumāreṇa
|| śivaṁ [||*]

inscription.head:
No. 252.
(A.R. No. 252 of 1908.)
KAṄKER, KANKER STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: On a boulder near the Mātā-guḍi in this village.

This Hindi record in Dēvanāgarī script may be assigned on
palaeographical considerations to about the 14th century.

It mentions one of the Kākaira chiefs Chaṁdrasēnadēvarāja belonging
to Kākēra-mahāsthāna.

inscription.head:
No. 254.
(A.R. No. 254 of 1908.)
KURUSPĀL, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: Slab built upside down in one of the steps of a tank called Rānī Tarai.

This record is undated but on palaeographical considerations it may
be assigned to c. 11th century. The cyclic year Saumya mentioned here
falls in Śaka 991 which corresponds to 1069 A.D.

The king Rājabhūshaṇa Sōmēśvaradēva is stated to have been born
of the Nāga race and bears all the epithets attributed to his name as in
the Nārāyanpāl inscription.

This slab is apparently dedicated to the god Kāmēśvara (Śiva) referred
to in the inscription. It records the grant of land near Kalamba by queens
Sōmala-mahādēvī and Dhāraṇa-mahādēvī. The land at Kalamba was given
to Kāmēśvara free of taxes and all encumbrances. Before it was endowed
to the god, it was made a dēvadāya (by) Paṁḍitā Taṭṭākatala-Mahaṇaka.
It mentions certain tenants, who are the residents of Nārāyaṇapura and
Ṭēmarā, as the witnesses.

It was caused to be engraved by D.mōdara Sūtradhāri. The verse was
composed by Tikama.

It is worthy of note that the queen Dhāraṇa-Mahādēvī and the names
of the recipients mentioned here also figure in the Nārāyanpāl record of
Guṇḍa-Mahādēvi. (See, Ep. Ind., Vol. IX, pp. 314-15).

Dhāraṇamahādēvī is no doubt mentioned as the queen (rāṇī) of
Sōmēśvaradēva in a fragmentary record from Kuruspāl (Ep. Ind., Vol. X,
p. 34; A.R.Ep., 1908, No. 260)

l.de: oṁ svasti [||*] sahasra phaṇamaṇi kirīṇa nikarāva bhāśva-
ma tabhaṭṭārakaḥ mahāmaheśvara ciraṇa kaṁja kiṁjalka-
yarājye saṁvatsaumya svasti gaṁgājala dhauta paramapavitrī ja-
mahādevī tasyā dharma[ḥ*] kalaṁmba samipasyā bhūmi dattaṁ de-
katala mahāmaka debadāya samaṁkṛtā bhūmi devasya dattaṁ jasya-
yasya jadā bhūmi tadā tasya tadā phalaṁ ṣaṣṭhiṁ variṣa sahasāṇi
svarge modaṁti bhūmidā mahānagahre ṭemarādhi dattaḥ sārinā-
yaka dhāthī nāyaka pragiḍā kāmaṭhi monaṇarāṇā doṇṭayo
• samasta dharmādhikaḥ visuvā korideva kāmeśvarasya dataḥ
grāmanāyakaḥ aṭavaṇṇi mahāparivra paṁḍita soma sama-
ṭṭamaṇa nāyaka gayādhara sādhumaidhara sādhu āmadeva jiṇa-
grāma sādhusomaṇa sidhudevu sādhu somā sādhu sahadeva siṁ-
• maha yugidarapa ekamaḥ nārāyaṇa pura vāsika bhami-
gā sādhu vighna bhoi palavā bhoi seṭhi mahaṇḍa brahmandha-
sādhu bhīmaḥ sargagakoṁ tasya suttaḥ dedu likhiṁtaṁ hisyāndi (?)
••• [vā] madhiṁkākṣara vā sarvva patipaṇṇamasu maṁgala mahā śrī
jo anyathā karoti tasya pitā gardabhaḥ sukarimātā
utkīritaṁ sūtradhārī dāmodara pajyaṁ racita tikama •••

inscription.head:
No. 255.
(A.R. No. 255 of 1908.)
KURUSPĀL, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p:
Slab fixed on the wall of palace building (Findspot: A tank known
as Choryā-tarāi).

This Sanskrit inscription in Nāgarī characters furnishes the details of
date viz., Śaka 1019, Īśvara, Saptamī, Saturday and Svāti-nakshatra. The cyclic
year Īśvara occurred in the Śaka year 1019 and not in Śaka 1020 as suggested
by Hiralal (Ep. Ind., Vol. X, p. 38). The tithi Saptamī combined with a
Saturday and the Svāti-nakshatra occurred in the bright fortnight of the
Śrāvaṇa month. The above details of date correspond to 1097 A.D., July
18, Saturday.

The record belongs to the victorious reign of the illustrious king
Sōmēśvaradēva who belonged to the Nāga family. He was the lord of
Bhōgavatī, whose crest was a cow and tiger, who was the Sun to the lotus
of the Chhinda family, who was a rāja-vidyā-nidhāna (store-house of
statesmanship), samasta-jagad-āśraya (the shelter of the whole world),
kōdaṇḍa Dhanaṁjaya (Arjuna in the use of bow), pratigaṇḍabhairava
(terrible to his opponents), raja-Purūrava (Purūravas among kings), bhōga-
vidyādhara (demi-gods in enjoyments), vīra-Lakshmī-dhara (brave like
Nārāyaṇa), mahim-Mahēṁdra (glorious like Indra), satya-Hariśchaṁdra,
Vindhyāvāsinīdēvī-varaprasāda (who had the favour of the blessings of
goddess Vindhyāvāsinī), etc.

It records the gift of eleven gadyāṇas for maintaining a lamp to the
deity Lōkēśvara by the residents of a village not named.

This was written by Chaṇḍēśvara-paṁḍita. It was caused to be engraved
by Mitradēva Sūtrakāra. Raṇadhavaḷa (Dugarā)ṇaka is also mentioned.

l.de: ya paṭu paṭāha gāṁbhīryya dhavālālaṁkārita daśā diśāṁtarā-
la dhenu vyāghra lāṁcchana cchiṁdakula kamala bhāskara mahāmahe-
śvara caraṇa kaṁja kiṁjalka puṁja piṁjarita bhramarāyamāna-
rāja vidhyā nidhāna samasta jegadāśrayaṁ kodaṁḍa dhanaṁjayaṁ rā-
jādhirāja sahaja manojaṁ pratigaṁḍa bhairava rāja pururava bho-
ga vidyādharaṁ vīra lakṣmīdharaṁ mahimā maheṁdra satya hariścaṁdraṁ

l.note: There is a symbol after in the word ‘gadyālanakā’

l.de: lākeśvarasya agreṇa dīpakaṁ yo dadāti vaiviśiṣṭaṁca
punyamanaṁtakaṁ || asya dharmma vināsāya kurute yo ma-
yāvannabho tiṣṭhati caṁdrasūryo meru[ḥ] kṣiti[rbhū] dharatārakādyāḥ

l.note: Read ‘pradānoyaṁ’ This does not suit the metre.

Compare with the last line of ‘Two Kuruspāl Inscriptions of DharaṇamahādēvI’
(See, Ep. Ind., Vol. X, p. 33, Text line 26).

inscription.head:
No. 256.
(A.R. No. 256 of 1908.)
KURUSPĀL, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: On a slab set up near the Chōrya tank in the village.

This Sanskrit record in Nāgarī characters of about the 13th century
mentions a certain nāyaka. The details are lost.

inscription.head:
No. 257.
(A.R. No. 257 of 1908.)
KURUSPĀL, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: On a slab found in the temple ruins in this village. (now at Jagadalpūr)

This Sanskrit record in Nāgarī characters may palaeographically be
assigned to the 11th century.

The king is credited with the birudas of the family to which he
belonged and they are similar to those found in the Nārāyaṇpāl inscription,
with little variations (See, Ep. Ind., Vol. IX, p. 311). This epigraph mentions
him as the son of Dhārāvarsha, whose grandson was Kanharadēva. It is
probable that Kanharadēva was a heir-apparent at the time of this record.

It refers to a number of countries like Uḍra, Lañji, Ratnapura, Lemṇa,
Vēṅgī, Bhadrapaṭṭaṇa and Vajra. But it is not clear whether king Sōmēśvara
had conquered kings of these countries as the inscription could not be
restored. They seem to be his rivals. Sōmēśvara is credited with the
achievements of the killing of the king Madhurāntaka in battle, burning
of Vajra country and the capture of six lakhs and 96 villages of Kōsala
country.

The inscription records grant of a village (name not clear) but bounded
by Indranadī in the south, village Āraṅgā in the east and Kapālika in the
west. The name of the village to the north is not clear. The river Indra
is the present Indrāvatī, about a mile from Kuruspāl, which flowed through
Chakrakūṭa. Chakrakūṭa, the sovereignty of which he acquired has been
identified by Hiralal with Chitrakūṭa, which may be a corruption of the
older name.
(Published in Ep. Ind., Vol. X, pp. 28-31).

l.note: The intended reading must be lakṣmyāḥ

l.de:
•• mā diśaḥ | nikhilāḥ samabhūdvārāvarṣanāmā naresvaraḥ | [1*] pautro
kanharadevo bhūttasya [rājño*]

ṣāditārātakaḥ harṣaṁ cātmajane pravarddhayati yaḥ saṁkaṁmalaṁbhūtale karṣatyeva
mahe[śvarasya*]

mahādevyāḥ jātaḥ someśvaronṛpaḥ | yogre na vodhavadbaddhayā nasavarṣo nṛpeṇa
[saḥ |] [5] ••••

taḥ | de[va][ḥ*] śropratigaṁḍabhairava iti khyātiṁ nṛpālaṁbha[nādru] drātmeti
ca rājabhūṣaṇa i[ti*] •••

[paga]cakro laṁjiratnapurapadmamahebhaḥ | [7*] nihatya vīro madhurāṁtakaṁ
nṛpaṁ mahāvalastaṁ [madhurā*]-

nibhaṁ raṇe tacārdaśtvā nṛpatī nivāsurānkumāra nārāyaṇatāmupāgataḥ |
[ca*] •• [na*]

hītale | [10*] lakṣāṣaṭkaṁ ṣaṇṇāvatyā sametaṁ grāmāṇāṁ yaḥ
kosalānāmahārṣīt | vī[vi]cārthaḥ •••

• vicāryo devācāryaḥ sauryamatijasāryaḥ | [11*] evamādiguṇa gaṇālaṁkṛto
mahārājādhi [rājapara]-

meśvara parama bhaṭṭārakaḥ śrīsomeśvaradevaḥ kadācidadha • prāyaṁ maraṇanimittakaṁ
[daṁḍaṁ] •••••

[tona] vihita cakrakūṭe anādiparaṁparayā varttamānaṁ śāśtradṛṣṭyā
samavasthitaṁ madvārṣa •••••

nānādeśakāṁ cāhutavānakāṁ syānini • t | nāmanī likhyate śrīpati
•• | śrīdhara | ma •••••

śrīmā[ha]va | dhādho goviṁdu | jajā | gāgū | • pati rāu | sāve
āhuṇḍa [gheṁkṛpu varasūdi] •••

[gādyā dhāna] | pathaṇāhārāhila | haradeva | kesava | cīkuma | dedū
| mādhava | śrīdhara | [supaṭu] ••••

[vāḍo] | mocivāḍo | kaṁsāravāḍo | evamādi kānvaṇigvarānbrāhmaṇān
āhūya dattavān para ••••

••• ••• rataraṁ sthānāṁca śukraṁ nāsti paṭikudavaṁ nāsti ita uttaraṁ
[maye ki] •• kaṭṭavila •••••

••• ••• [naghanapa grāma]ḥ pūrvvasyāṁ diśi āraṁgā | dakṣiṇasyā miṁdranado
| pratīcyāṁ kapālika •••••

••• ••• nigrāmaḥ etanmadhye yaḥ kaściddeśāṁtaroya • [nyakaḥ] bhāgayuktaḥ
••• kaḥ •••

••• ••• tiṣṭati sa sarvodisi madhyavartī tasyāpyayameva dharmaḥ | atrārthe
sākṣiṇaḥ mahāpradhānaḥ ka •••••

••• mada daṁnāyakaḥ rāmanaḥ saṁdhivigrahināyakaḥ mānavasāha |
adhipatirāutaḥ yasa •••

maṁyo rakṣati tasyāpi mahadeva puṇyaṁ | vahubhirvasudhā | dattā rājabhiḥ
sagarādibhiḥ [| yasya ya*]-

[sya*] yadā bhūmistasya tasya tadā phalamiti [12*] yastu nāiyati
sa prayāga vārāṇasyādi ••••

•••• [dha] brāhmaṇavadhādi pātakaṁ prā • vaṁti [|*] svadattāṁ paradattāṁ
vāyo hareta vasuṁdharā [|*] ṣa[ṣṭiṁ varṣa sa*]-

hasrāṇi viṣṭāyāṁ jāyate kṛmiḥ || [13*] raṇadhavalarupakāreṇa utkīritaṁ
||

inscription.head:
No. 258.
(A.R. No. 258 of 1908.)
JAGDALPŪR, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: On a broken slab to the west of the village Kuruspāl. (now at Jagdalpūr).

This Sanskrit inscription in Nāgarī characters is badly damaged. Only
the imprecatory portion is preserved.

inscription.head:
No. 259.
(A.R. No. 259 of 1908.)
TĒMARĀ, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: On a sati-stone found in the temple ruins.

This buried stone inscription is in Sanskrit language and Nāgarī
characters. It may palaeographically be assigned to c. 14th century.

The record is dated in the Śaka year 1246, Raktākshi, Chaitra śu [12],
śanau (Saturday). The cyclic year Raktākshi fell in the Śaka year 1247,
which was current. The tithi dvādaśi does not coincide with the month
Chaitra and the week-day Saturday. Therefore, the tithi was navami on
that day. However, the details of date work out to Saturday, the 23rd March,
1325 A.D. It records the immolation of the Māṇikyadēvī after the death
of her husband at Ṭēmarā-sthāna of Sairaharāja-rājya in Chakrakūṭa-rāshṭra.

The epigraph does not give any clue as to what family king
Hariśchaṁdra belonged. But it is not impossible that he belonged to the
Chhinda or Sinda family of the Nāga race who ruled during the 11th-
12th centuries. The reasons for this inference are: 1. Chakrakūṭa was situated
in Bastar and the fact that Ṭēmrā is a village adjoining Kuruspāl being
included in its rāshṭra (i.e., Chakrakūṭa-rāshṭra) during the reign of king
Hariśchandra. and 2. The name of his (kings) wife Māṇikyadēvī was so
named after the goddess Māṇikyadēvī, worshipped by the kings of
Nāgavaṁśi family. This is an ancient name of Dantēśvarī so named by
the successors of the Nāgavaṁśis, the Kākatīyas. The Bhairamgārh
inscription contains the birudas similar to those found in the Bārsūr one,
and the king is stated in both to be the worshipper of Māṇikyadēvī (śrī-
Māṇikyadēvī-divya-śrīpāda-padm-ārādhaka) (Ep. Ind., Vol. IX p. 164; Ibid.,
Vol. III, p. 318). The Nāgavaṁśi rulers seem to have worshipped Durgā
under the name Māṇikyadēvī (Ep. Ind., Vol. X. p. 27, foot note 7).

It mentions the glorious (god) Kalaṁkāṁ Nārāyaṇa residing at
Mahāgōshṭha and Āmaṇa, the executive officer (dēśakāri) of Hariśchaṁdradēva.

l.de: rṇa loke gate tat sakī satī

inscription.head:
No. 260.
(A.R. No. 260 of 1908.)
KURUSPĀL, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: On a slab in a field in this village.

This Sanskrit record in Nāgarī script may palaeographically be assigned
to 11th century and thus seems to be ancient than the record from Nārāyaṇpāl
dated Śaka 1033 (See, Ep. Ind., Vol. IX, pp. 311 ff and plate).

It records the grant of land situated near Kalēmva, free from all
encumbrances and taxes to the deity Kāmēśvaradēva by Dhāraṇa-mahādēvī,
queen of King Sōmēśvaradēva, who assumed the epithet Rājabhūshaṇa.
Since the grant lands by the above queen mentioned in this place and dated
in the reign period of the same king are from one and the same village
Kadamba it is possible that these two records are not far away from date.
The latter record from Kuruspāl is dated in the cyclic year Saumya falling
in Śaka 991. Perhaps, our record may be placed around this date, if not
earlier, on the basis of the grant details.
(Published in Ep. Ind., Vol. X, p. 34, Text B).

edition.l: ṇa [|*] jasya Read ‘yasya’ yasya jadā Read ‘yadā’ bhūmi Read ‘yadā’-

inscription.head:
No. 261.
(A.R. No. 261 of 1908.)
NĀRĀYAṆPĀL, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: On a slab set up in front of the temple of Nārāyaṇa.

This Sanskrit epigraph in Nāgarī characters furnishes the details of
date viz., Śaka 1033, Khara, Kārttika, full-moon day, Wednesday
corresponding to 1111 A.D., October 18.

It records the grant of the village Nārāyaṇapura to the god Nārāyaṇa
and some land near the Kharjuri tank to the god Lōkēśvara by Guṇḍa-
mahādēvī, the chief queen of the Mahārāja Dhārāvarsha, the mother of
Sōmēśvaradēva and the grandmother of Kanharadēva, who was then ruling
after the death of his father. The land is stated to have been endowed
free from all encumbrances on the village, the nilaya (dwelling group) and
the nāḍu.

It states that the lands of the minister Ādityama Penna, the
bhaṭṭanāyaka Prōlliyō, the karaṇa Kudriyama Virama, the nāyakas Sōmana,
Rāmanākāsana, Mārayā, the bhaṇḍāri Aitana and the sādhu Vakōmarya
were given revenue free. The śrēshṭhin Samara, the brāhmaṇa Chhādrū,
the śrēshṭhin Ghughi, the sādhu Śrīdhara, the kōsajā (cocoon producer)
Nānū, the mālī Pālāsu Padmāsinivīra the oilman (tailika) Gōvindapātra, the
vāvā (bābū) Jasadhavala Milāpi and others are referred to as gṛihavēṭakas
and Purushōttama as the illustrious paṇḍit. Also land was granted free from
taxes to Sūtradhāra Raṇavirāya.

Further, the lands of adhikāri Chhādrūka, Brahmāujhāka, Mahaṇuka,
Kanharasāi and Nāgakuladvara are granted free from taxes. Some land
was endowed tax-free. Some land was endwoed tax-free to Bhaṭṭāraka
Bhāvadēva.

It records, in addition to the above grants, grant of land to the deity
Nārāyaṇa, free from taxes. A number of persons are referred to as the
witnesses to the above grant. One of the witnesses named Mahaṇaka
Dēvadāsa also figures in the Kuruspāl inscription of Dhāraṇa-mahādēvī of
the time of Sōmēśvara (See Ep. Ind., Vol. X, p. 33, Textline 12).
(Published in Ep. Ind., Vol. IX, pp. 311 ff and plate facing p. 314).

inscription.head:
No. 262.
(A.R. No. 262 of 1908.)
NĀRĀYAṆPĀL, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: On a slab in the field of the village (now at Jagadalpur).

This record may be assigned on paleographical grounds to c. 12th
century.

It states that the field where the inscribed stone is found belongs to
the temple of Śrī Rudrēśvara.

inscription.head:
No. 263.
(A.R. No. 263 of 1908.)
SIHĀWĀ RAIPUR DISTRICT, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: Central shrine in the kannarēśvara temple.

This Sanskrit inscription in Nāgarī script do not contain any other details
of date except Śaka year 1114.

The inscription gives the genealogy of the king from Siṁharāja. This
inscription records the construction of five temples, two in his parents name,
two in his own, and one in his issueless brother's name by king Karṇarāja
of Kākaira and of one by his wife, queen Bhōpalladevi. These were all
built at the sacred place Dēvahrada. The inscription was written in the year
1191-92 A.D. apparently after the completion of the whole group of temples,
and it was put up in one of the king's own temples in which Śiva was
enshrined. The other one of those which he had built for himself was
dedicated to Kēśava.

This inscription gives the genealogy of the king Karṇarāja after whom
the temple is still called Kanīsar or Karṇēśvara.

The writer of the inscription was Sūtradhāra Sūpā and the composer
of the praśasti was Nṛisiṁha.
(see Ep. Ind. Vol. IX, pp. 185 ff.)

inscription.head:
No. 264.
(A.R. No. 264 of 1908.)
SORAR, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: On a slab lying to the east of the village.
This damaged record mentions śrī Mūladēva. Other details are lost.

inscription.head:
No. 265.
(A.R. No. 265 of 1908.)
SUNĀRPĀL, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: On a slab set up under a tree in the village.

This Sanskrit inscription in Nāgarī characters may be assigned to 12th
century on palaeographical grounds.

It does not furnish any date. It records the grant of the village
Ādhakāḍa by king Jeyasiṁhadēva of the Nāga race, probably in the presence
of his two queens Lōkamahādēvī and the great queen Sāmaṁnadēvī and
the five ministers (pañchapradhāna) The two witnesses to this grant are
Aṁvāvali-nāyaka and Naḍayā-Brāhmaṇa. The five ministers include the
mahāpradhāni Namava-nāyaka, the paḍivāla, chavari-kumara, sarvvavādi-
nāyaka (the officer of the intelligence department) and another named
Dōdēsari-pātra Chavākā.
This was caused to be written by Pulana-nāyaka.

The term paḍivāla is interesting. It may be synonymous to the
expression paḍaivēlan in Tamil meaning an officer of rank in the army.
(Published in Epigraphia Indica; Vol. X, pp. 35-36).

inscription.head:
No. 266.
(A.R. No. 266 of 1908.)
TĪRTHGARH, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: On a slab resting on a rock near the falls.
This epigraph may palaeographically be assigned to c. 14th century.
It reads Hēmatasī-hadavāta.

inscription.head:
No. 267.
(A.R. No. 267 of 1908.)
TĪRTHGARH, BASTAR STATE, CENTRAL PROVINCES.
inscription.p: On a slab resting on a rock near the falls.

This Sanskrit inscription in Nāgarī of c. 19th century, mentions one
Hematasīha.

inscription.head:
No. 268.
(A.R. No. 268 of 1908.)
KARIVAḶAMVANDANALLŪR, SANKARANAYINAR KOYIL TALUK,
TIRUNELVELI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: North wall of the central shrine in the Pālvaṇṇanāthasvāmi temple.

p.b:
Tribhº Kōnērinmaikoṇḍāṉ Śiṟi Perumāḷ
Śīvalamāṟavar Guṇarāmaṉ alias Pāṇḍya
Kulaśēkhara-Dīṭshakar Śaka 1574: Regnal year 37.

inscription.p:
This inscription is dated in the 37th year of the reign of the above
king. The other details of date viz., Śaka 1574, Nandana, Āvaṇi 4 correspond
to 1652 A.D., August 4, (Wednesday).

It records the grant of land to Vararāma Vikkiṟamapāṇḍiya-
Mūrkuḍikōvaikāṟaṉ, the śrībaṇḍārak-kāriyañcheyvār of the temple of
Tirukkaḷāvuḍaiya-tambirāṉār, for the kaṭṭaḷai, in lieu of 25 poṉ given by
him for the performance of worship to the deity. It is stated that Gaṇapati-
paṇḍāram was to conduct the above mentioned kaṭṭaḷai. The details of
lands at Karuvarainallūr given by the śrīkāryam-cheyvār are mentioned.

inscription.head:
No. 269.
(A.R. No. 269 of 1908.)
KARIVAḶAMVANDANALLŪR, SANKARANAYINAR KOYIL TALUK,
TIRUNELVELI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: South wall of the central shrine in the Pālvaṇṇanāthasvāmi temple.

This inscription furnishes the details of date viz., Śaka 1572, Vikṛiti,
Vaikāśi 6 corresponding to 1650 A.D., May 4.

It records the grant of land by śrībaṇḍārak-kāriyam-cheyvār of the
temple of Tirukkaḷāvuḍaiya-tambirāṉār to meet the expenses of the festivals
on the fourth day both to the above deity and the goddess, festival to be
conducted to Chaṇḍeśuraru, for the pradōsha-kaṭṭaḷai to be conducted
monthly and for the deity to be taken in procession; to the maṭha.

l.ta: rkku viṭṭapaṭiyiṉāle yivar anta nilattaik ko-

inscription.head:
No. 270.
(A.R. No. 270 of 1908.)
KARIVAḶAMVANDANALLŪR, SANKARANAYINAR KOYIL TALUK,
TIRUNELVELI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: South wall of the central shrine in the Pālvaṇṇanāthasvāmi temple.

The details of date viz., Śaka 1324, regnal year 2+5, Paṅguni are not
sufficient for verification.

It records the grant of 36 mā of land at Teṉkaraikuṟuchchi in Āri-
nāḍu excluding the old dēvadāṉa lands of the deity by the king for the
daily worship and food offerings to the deity Tirukkaḷādīchchuram-uḍaiya-
nāyanār of Karuvaranallūr in Ari-nāḍu. The gift was entrusted to the śrī-
Rudra śrī-Mahēśvarañ-cheyvār of the above temple who undertook to
maintain the worship from out of the income from lands by raising crops
through tenants.

The same was ordered to be deducted from the accounts of the variyilār.
Tachchaimēli-uḍaiyāṉ figures as a witness to the taxes collected from the
village Karivaranallūr. This village is identical with Karivaḷamvandanallūr.

inscription.head:
No. 271.
(A.R. No. 271 of 1908.)
KARIVAḶAMVANDANALLŪR, SANKARANAYINAR KOYIL TALUK,
TIRUNELVELI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: South wall of the central shrine in the Pālvaṇṇanāthasvāmi temple.

p.b:
Tribhº Kōnērinmaikoṇḍāṉ Iṟandakālam-
eḍutta Perumāḷ Śrīvallabha Śaka 1466, year 10:

inscription.p:
This incomplete inscription furnishes the details of date viz., Śaka 1466,
Regnal year 10, Krōdhi, Ṟishabha 2, ba 3. In the absence of the weekday
and nakshatra, the christian equivalent cannot be worked out.

The king is stated to be the son of Abhirāma Parākramapāṇḍyadēva.
He is credited with the title Tirunelvēlip-perumāḷ. It records the gift to
the deity Tirukkiḷāvu[ḍaiya*]-nayiṉār for hōma and abhishēka by the king.
Other details are not known as the record stops abruptly.

konerimai
koṇṭāṉ iṟantakālam eṭutta perumāḷ śrī

pacumukkālu vikiṟama pāṇṭiyakkoṉu caṭaiyakkoṉ pacu
oṉṟu aḻakaṉ periyāṉ nallāḷ koṉ •••••

inscription.head:
No. 272.
(A.R. No. 272 of 1908.)
KARIVAḶAMVANDANALLŪR, SANKARANAYINAR KOYIL TALUK,
TIRUNELVELI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: North wall of the first prākāra of the Pālvaṇṇanāthasvāmi temple.

p.b:
Chaḍaiyavarmaṉ Tribhº Kōnērin-
maikoṇḍāṉ Abhirāma Varatuṅga-
rāma Vīrapāṇḍya Śaka 1511: 1588 A.D.

inscription.p: This epigraph commences with a Sanskrit verse invoking Śiva.

The details of date viz., Śaka 1511, regnal year 2, Sarvadhāri,
dakshiṇāyana, Dhanu-Ravi 6 Wednesday, ēkādaśi, Svāti-nakshatra corresponding
to 1588 A.D., December 4.

It records the grant of Vaṅgaikuḷam in Mallayampaṭṭu in Āriya-nāḍu
as kuḍinīṅg-dēvadāṉa to Śeṇbagavaṉap-perumāḷ Irāmīchchuraṉ, who has to
expend annually 20 paṇam to meet the expenses of the birth-day celebration
of the king on the star of Pūsa falling in the month of Puraṭṭāśi.

An order (ōlai) was issued to this effect by the vāśalttāṉigar to the
kaṇakkar (accountant) and drafted in the name of Ādichaṇḍēśvaran.

inscription.head:
No. 273.
(A.R. No. 273 of 1908.)
KARIVAḶAMVANDANALLŪR, SANKARANAYINAR KOIL TALUK,
TIRUNELVELI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: North wall of the first prākāra of the Pālvaṇṇanāthasvāmi temple.

p.b:
Abhirāma-Varatuṅgarāma Vīra-pāṇḍya Śaka 1480, Regnal year 8
= 1558 A.D.

inscription.p:
The record furnishes the details of date viz., Kāḷayukti, Āvaṇi 22, ba
3, Tuesday, Pūraṭṭādi.

This inscription begins with the praśasti of the king Śrīmat
Sushēṇanāthasya-mukhaliṁgasya, of the king. The king bears the surname
Jaṭilavarmman Tribhuvanachchakravartti, Kōnērmaikoṇḍāṉ and title like
Bhuvanēkavīraṉ, chandrakuladīpaṉ, Madhurāmahēndra, Jayanthamaṅgala-
puravarādhīśvara, Kshēmāsuranārasiṁha, Kēraḷatamōdivākara,
Sindhuvaṭhavānala, etc.

It refers to an order of the king (nam-kumaraṉ) recording the assignment
of kāṇiyāṭchai in the name of deity Viśvanāthan, after changing the
paṇḍuḍaiyār and the old name. Also mentions the accountant (nam-kāriyam)
Parākrama-Pāṇḍya, etc.

inscription.head:
No. 274.
(A.R. No. 274 of 1908.)
KARIVAḶAMVANDANALLŪR, SANKARANAYINAR KOYIL TALUK,
TIRUNELVELI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: North wall of the first prākāra of the Pālvaṇṇanāthasvāmi temple.

This incomplete record furnishes the details of date viz., Śaka 1469,
regnal year 4, Plavaṅga, Vaikāśi ba 3, Monday, Śubha-yōga, Ṟishabha-karaṇa
and Aśvati-nakshatra.

It seems to record a gift as kāṇi-āṭchi by Māṇikkaṉ to Perum-
Villavarāyaṉ Tiru[viḻalam] Kāṅgīyaṉ of Irāśēndiram.

The record further mentions the various titles of a king whose name
is not given. The epithets like Bhuvanēkavīra, Chandrakulapradīpa,
Jayanthamaṅgala-puravarādhīśvara, etc., are found attributed to king
Aḻagaṉ Perumāḷ Parākramapāṇḍya, the father of Chaḍaiyavarmaṉ
Tribhuvanachakravarti Kōnērinmaikoṇḍāṉ Tirunelvēli[perumāḷ*], who was
born in the nakshatra Aśvati (Aśvini) in a record of the same date. Hence,
the king who bears similar epithets in the record under review may be
identified with Aḻagaṉ Perumāḷ Parākrama-pāṇḍya. (See A.R.Ep., 1908, No.
277).

inscription.head:
No. 275.
(A.R. No. 275 of 1908.)
KARIVAḶAMVANDANALLŪR, SANKARANAYINAR KOYIL TALUK,
TIRUNELVELI DISTRICT.

p.b:
Abhirāma Varatuṅga Śaka 1517, Regnal year 10 = 1595 A.D.
Vīra Pāṇḍya.

inscription.p:
The details of date given in the record viz., Maṉmata, Aṟpaśi 25 do
not admit for verification. It refers to the institution of abhishēkha-Kaṭṭaḷai
to the deity Tirukkaḷāvuḍaiya-tambirāṉār and mentions a certain Pāṇḍyadēva
(name lost).

inscription.head:
No. 276.
(A.R. No. 276 of 1908.)
KARIVAḶAMVANDANALLŪR, SANKARANAYINAR KOYIL TALUK,
TIRUNELVELI DISTRICT.

p.b:
Jaṭilavarman Tribhuvana-chakravartti Śaka 1472
Kōnēriṉmaikaṇḍāṉ Perumāḷ Kulaśēkharadēva.

inscription.p:
The record is dated in the Śaka year 1472 which along with other
details of date viz., Piṅgaḷa, Paṅguṉi 15 ba 7, Wednesday and Viśakha-
nakshatra.

It records the grant of lands at Karivaranallūr in Āri-nāḍu as kāṇi-
āṭchai in the name of Ativīrarāmaṉ.

inscription.head:
No. 277.
(A.R. No. 277 of 1908.)
KARIVAḶAMVANDANALLŪR, SANKARANAYINAR KOYIL TALUK,
TIRUNELVELI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: North wall of the first prākāra of the Pālvaṇṇanāthasvāmi temple.

This inscription furnishes the details of date viz., Śaka 1469, year 5,
Plavaṅga, Kārttigai, ••• 3, Monday, Tiruvādirai-nakshatra.

It seems to record the assignment of tiruvilāñchiṉai and karuvēlagam,
obtained as kāṇiyāṭchai, to Āṇḍukoṇḍunayiṇāṉ Kaḍaiyōgakāttāṉ of
Aḷḷikuṉṟa Mārttaṉūr-udaiyāṉ in Vaḍamuṭṭa-nāḍu.

The details of the grant are lost as it is incomplete. The division
Vaḍamuṭṭa-nāḍu figures in a record of Āttūr in Tiruchchendur taluk (Cf.
S.I.I., Vol. XIV, No. 214).

The king is stated to have been the son of Aḻagaṉ Perumāḷ Parākrama-
pāṇḍyadēva, who bears a string of epithets in the present record and also
mentioned to have been born on the day of Aśvati (Aśvini)-nakshatra.

inscription.head:
No. 278.
(A.R. No. 278 of 1908.)
KARIVAḶAMVANDANALLŪR, SANKARANAYINAR KOYIL TALUK,
TIRUNELVELI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: North wall of the kitchen in the Pālvaṇṇanāthasvāmi temple.

This incomplete epigraph is dated in Śaka 1393, Makara 25 śu 10.
Tuesday, Mṛigaśīrsha-nakshatra.

It seems to record the royal grant of six mā-kāṇi-mundirigaiyiṉ-araiyē-
araikkāl of land for six house-sites (maṉai) in the village [Tā]varanallūr in
Āri-nāḍu to six chaturvēdi-bhaṭṭas belonging to the different gōtras and
sūtras residing in the village Śrīvallabhach-chaturvēdimaṅgalam on the day
of his natal-star Sadaiyam falling on the 26th day of the month Ṛishabha
in the Śaka year 1392.

The details of the six shares apportioned to the brāhmaṇas, the
boundaries of the lands endowed to them and the names of the donees
are mentioned. A street named Āgavarāmaṉ-perunteruvu probably named
after a king who assumed the title Āhavarāmaṉ is mentioned as one of
the boundaries of the lands endowed.

inscription.head:
No. 279.
(A.R. No. 279 of 1908.)
KARIVAḶAMVANDANALLŪR, SANKARANAYINAR KOYIL TALUK,
TIRUNELVELI DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On the inner gōpura, left of entrance, of the temple of
Pālvaṇṇanāthasvāmi.

This damaged record may be assigned on palaeographical grounds to
c. 15th century.

It seems to record an order (ōlai) of a king, whose name is not
mentioned, granting 50 paṇam to meet the expenses of the daily worship
and food offerings to the deity Ṉandīśvaram-uḍaiya-nayiṉār on the eleventh
day in the month of Chittirai of the year Vikṛiti. The details of the grant
portion are not clear.

l.ta: ku nāḷ oṉṟukku amutupaṭi irunāḻiyāka mā-

inscription.head:
No. 280.
(A.R. No. 280 of 1908.)
KARIVAḶAMVANDANALLŪR, SANKARANAYINAR KOYIL TALUK,
TIRUNELVELI DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
On the inner gōpura, right of entrance of the temple of
Pālvaṇṇanāthasvāmi.

The Tamil record in verse may be palaeographically assigned to c.
century. It registers the construction of the gōpura of the temple of
Pālvaṇṇanāthar.

inscription.head:
No. 281.
(A.R. No. 281 of 1908.)
SAṄKARANAYIṈĀR KŌYIL, SANKARANAYINAR KOYIL TALUK,
TIRUNELVELI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: North wall of the Gōmati-ammaṉ shrine in the Śaṅkaranayiṉār temple.

p.b:
Chaḍaiyavarmaṉ Perumāḷ Daṉmaperumāḷ alias Kulaśēkharadēva
Year 3 and śaka 1475: 1553 A.D.

inscription.p:
This record furnishes the details of date viz., Śaka 1475, regnal year
3, Pramādīcha, Ṟishabha ba 7, Thursday, Aviṭṭa-nakshatra corresponding to
1553 A.D., May 4.

It refers to his son Aḻagaṉ Perumāḷ who was born on the day of the
star Mūla and records an order (ōlai) issued by the king to kāriyam-nāyaṉ,
kāriyakaṇakku-piḻai-poṟukkum-perumāṇ, Toṇḍargaḷ-kaṇakku śaḍai-uḍaiyāṉ,
kaṉmigaḷ, ālaiyakkaṇakku vaḷatturu-vākki and kaḍaikūṭṭu-kaṇakku Kulaśēkara-
Maḻavarāyaṇ for the grant of 2 mā of lands, adding the kaṇḍuḻavu opened
up for taxation as kāṇiyāṭchai. It states that one mā of land was exempted
from the accounts of Variyilār and endowed for the house-site. The details
of the boundaries of the house-site (maṉai) and the area of kaṇḍuḻavu lands
at Uttamanallūr in Kōṭṭūr-nāḍu and Paṟṟūr in Āri-nāḍu are specified.

inscription.head:
No. 282.
(A.R. No. 282 of 1908.)
SAṄKARANAYIṈĀR KŌYIL, SANKARANAYINAR KOYIL TALUK,
TIRUNELVELI DISTRICT.
inscription.p: Slab lying in front of the Śaṅkaranayiṉār temple.

This inscription furnishes the details of date viz., Kali 4962, Śaka 1783,
Kollam 1036, Duṟmati, Aṟpaśi, Wednesday śu 13, Uttiraṭṭādi-nakshatra
corresponding to 1861 A.D., October 16, ·34.

It states that under the orders of Tāhsīldār Gōvindaṉāyakkar the utsava
images of goddess, sabhāṇāyakkar, etc., were caused to be cast as per the
āgamic conception and śilpaśāstra when the jeṭibandana and reconsecration
ceremony are stated to have been performed. A silver pedestal for procession
was prepared and the makara festival was conducted for the prosperity and
growth of the heirs of the above nāyakkar.
This was written by Vaikuṇṭa Aḷagappaṉāchāri.

The details of date when the reconsecration was done correspond to
1862 A.D., February 3, f.d.t. ·11.

inscription.head:
No. 283.
(A.R. No. 283 of 1908.)
AḶAGĀDRIPUTTŪR, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: North wall of the central shrine in Svarṇapurīśvara temple.

In the absence of the regular praśasti of the king, the record may
be assigned to Rājarāja I on the basis of his title ‘Rājarājakēsarivarman’,
with which the record commences.

The record furnishes the details of date viz., regnal year 7, Kaṟkaṭaka,
Monday, saptami, Sōdi (Svāti)-nakshatra corresponding to 991 A.D., July 20,
f.d.n. ·05.

It records an iṟai-kāval-oṭṭōlai issued by the peruṅguṟi-perumakkaḷ
which met at the Pērambalam of the above village to the temple of
Tirupputtūruḍaiya-Paramasvāmi of Pāradāyakuḍi, a brahmadēya in Tirunaṟaiyūr-
nāḍu.

It refers to the construction of the śrīvimāna to the deity Paramasvāmi
and registers the grant of mā-kāṇi of maḍakku lands below the channel
called Āyirattu-muṉṉūṟṟuva-vākkāl, made after purchase for a price-value
of sixty kaḻañju of tuḷaippoṉ, by Pullālich-chāttaṉ Kārivēḷar Gaṇḍarādittaṉ
alias Mummaḍiśōḻiyavaraiyar to provide for the food-offerings and perpetual
lamp to the deity Paramasvāmi. It is stated that the dēvarkaṉmigaḷ undertook
to collect the various taxes such as vāśalil pōnda-kuḍimai, ūriḍuvarippāḍum-
iṟai, ēchchōṟu, seṉṉīrveṭṭi, etc., on these lands. These iṟaikāval-oṭṭōlai was
drafted by madhyasthan Mādāṉamaṅgalam-uḍaiyāṉ Pusa •• gaṉ Gōvindaṉ
alias Irunūṟṟuva Alaṅkārapriyaṉ.

Besdies a stand lamp (nilai-viḷakku) of five span length instituted by
Vēḷaṉ Gaṇḍarā[di*]ch-chaṉ alias Mummuḍichōḷa-Śōḻiyavaraiyar, he is
referred to have endowed a śēkaṇḍi (spouted vessel) of 110 niṟai and a
bell (mūvaṇaik-kaimmaṇi) of 187 niṟai, amounting to 297 niṟai.

The surnames Gaṇḍarādittaṉ and Mummaḍiśōḻiyavaraiyar must have
been assumed by Kārivēḷar after king Gaṇḍarāditya, father of Uttama-chōḷa
and Rājarāja I respectively. Pāradāyakkuḍi was perhaps a brāhmaṇa quarter
of Tirupputtūr, the same as the modern Aḷagādri-puttūr where the village
assembly existed and met.

inscription.head:
No. 284.
(A.R. No. 284 of 1908.)
AḶAGĀDRIPUTTŪR, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: North wall of the central shrine in Svarṇapurīśvara temple.

This damaged inscription registers the gift of money for maintaining
sandhi daily to the deity probably of Pāradāyakuḍi, a brahmadēya in
Tirunaṟaiyūr-nāḍu in •••• khāmaṇi-vaḷanāḍu. The names of the donors
and other details of the gift are not clear.

inscription.head:
No. 285.
(A.R. No. 285 of 1908.)
AḶAGĀDRIPUTTŪR, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: South wall of the central shrine in Svarṇapurīśvara temple.

This incomplete record may palaeographically be assigned to c. 12th
century.

The epigraph commences with the praśasti Pūmādu puṇara, etc., of
the king and records the gift of money for maintaining a sandhi lamp to
the deity Aḻagiyadēva of Tirupputtūr. Mention is made of śaṅkarappāḍi.
The details of the grant portion and the name of the donor are not found.

inscription.head:
No. 286.
(A.R. No. 286 of 1908.)
AḶAGĀDRIPUTTŪR, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: South wall of the central shrine in Svarṇapurīśvara temple.

This epigraph is in three pieces, of which two are fragments.
Palaeographically they may be assigned to c. 11th century.

The first two of the three peices relate to the gift of lamps to the
deity Aḻagiyadēva of Tirupputtūr. In the first instance, Pukkamuchchāṉi
Umaiyāṇḍāḷ and brāhmaṇi Uyyavandāḷ are referred to as the donatrix. The
second fragment records the endowment of land for the tirunundāviḷakku-
puṟam by the ūravar during the reign period of Tribhuvanachakravarti
Kulōttuṅgachōḷa. The next piece refers to the ūravar of Māṅgāḍu, whose
names are mentioned unlike the second piece and registers some land grant.
Mentions Aḻagar Tirupputtūr in Kulōttuṅgaśōḻa-vaḷanāḍu.

inscription.head:
No. 287.
(A.R. No. 287 of 1908.)
AḶAGĀDRIPUTTŪR, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: East wall of the central shrine in Svarṇapurīśvara temple.

This damaged inscription begins with the praṁasti, Tirumagaḷ-pōlap-
perunilach-chelviyum, etc., of the king.

It records the grant of three mā of lands made tax-free (iṟaiyili) by
the sabhai of Pā[ra*]dā[ya*]kuḍi, a brahmadēya in Tirunaraiyūr-nāḍu in
Kshatriyaśikhāmaṇi-vaḷanāḍu by obtaining eight kāśu from Māṭilan Sōman
Sūryyan of the above village. The details of the boundaries of the land
endowed are specified.

This was drafted by madhyasthan of this village Vēṅkaṭavan Nārāyaṇan
alias Āyirattumuṉṉūṟṟuvan under the orders of Pāradāyan Pudanārāyaṇan
and Pāradāyan Kēśavan.

inscription.head:
No. 288.
(A.R. No. 288 of 1908.)
AḶAGĀDRIPUTTŪR, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: East wall of the central shrine in Svarṇapurīśvara temple.

This incomplete inscription commences with the praśasti, Pugaḻ-śūḻnda-
puṇari, etc., of the king. The date of the record is lost. The details of
date viz., Tulā and Sunday are not sufficient for verification.

It records the sale of 7 mā of lands by the peruṅguṟi-perumakkaḷ of
a brahmadēya village in Tirunaraiyūr-nāḍu to a person (whose name is not
clear) belonging to a place included in Uṟṟukkāṭṭu-kōṭṭam, who granted the
same to provide for different food-offerings during the midday service. The
grant thus made, probably to the deity of this place, was exempted from
taxes (iṟai-iḻichchi) The peruṅguṟi-perumakkaḷ obtained 13 kāśu as the price-
value for the sale of lands made. A number of signatories who attested
the sale document are mentioned.

••• ••• •••• ntāmotiraṉ i[ rājendra] coḻa[ṉā]na
coḷentira ciṅka branmamārāya neḻuttinālum ivvūr

inscription.head:
No. 289.
(A.R. No. 289 of 1908.)
AḶAGĀDRIPUTTŪR, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: East and South walls of the central shrine in Svarṇapurīśvara temple.

This record furnishes the details of date viz., year 4, Kuṁbha, Thursday
and Aṉiḻa (Anusha-nakshatra).

It states that Paṭṭālagaṉ Ādittaṉ of Kallūr in Mēl-Vemba-nāḍu in Pāṇḍi-
nāḍu alias Rājaraja-maṇḍalam raised a shrine to the deity Sūryadēva in
the Śrīkōyil of Tirupputtūr. The sabhai of Pāradāyakuḍi (peruṅguṟi-
perumakkaḷ), a brahmadēya in Tirunaṟaiyūr-nāḍu in Kshatriyaśikhāmaṇi-
vaḷanāḍu which met at Śrīkurugūr-ma[ṉ*]ṟam, situated to the north of the
village tank, decided to sell six mā of lands after obtaining the price-value
of 30 kāśu and endowed the same after exempting it from taxes (iṟai-iḻichchi)
to provide for the different food-offerings to the above deity.

The details of the boundaries of the gift land including the land
mentioned as the uvachchappuṟam of the deity Tirunaraiyūrdēva, are
specified. The lands yielded sixty kalam of paddy. The ends of lines 7
to 11 are lost.

group.h3:
No. 290-290A.
(A.R. No. 290 and 290A of 1908.)
ĀṆḌĀṈKŌYIL, PAPANASAM TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
group.p: East, South and west walls of the goddess shrine in a Śiva temple.

There are two pieces in this place. The first peice which is incomplete
furnishes the details of date viz., year 22, Kaṉṉi ba 13, Saturday, Uttira-
nakshatra corresponding to 1318 A.D., September 23.

It records the grant of lands purchased as maḍakkāṇi from
Tiruveṇkāṭṭu-mudaliyār of the lineage of Pichchai-maḍam by the tāṉattār,
māhēśvarar, nāḍu, nagara, Kōmuṟṟamar (king ?) and samaiya-kāriyattukku-
kaḍavar, to the dēvakaṉmis of the temple of Aṇḍa-nāyaṉār as tirunāmattuk-
kāṇi. The boundaries of tirunāmattuk-kāṇi lands are given in detail in the
second piece. The details are not known as the ends of lines are lost.

inscription.head:
No. 291.
(A.R. No. 291 of 1908.)
TIRUKKAḶITTAṬṬAI, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: North wall of the central shrine in the Vēdapurīśvara temple.

The inscription begins with the meykīrtti, Pāṇḍiyaṉai-churam iṟakkiṉa
(who drove the Pāṇḍya king into the forest.) and registers a grant made
by his general Pirāntakaṉ Śiṟiyavēḷaṉ alias Tirukkaṟṟaḷi Pichchaṉ to provide
for offerings to the temple of Śrīkuḍitiṭṭai in Vēmbāṟṟūr on the northern
bank (of the Kāvērī). The donor is stated to have purchased for 156 kaḻañju
of gold, a piece of land measuring half a vēli and made it rent-free before
it was endowed to the temple.
Published in Ep. Ind., Vol. XII, pp. 121 ff.

pāṇṭiyaṉai curam miṟakkina perumāḷ śrī
cu

ttukku vacakku taḷi viḷāka [m*]eṉṟu perkūvappaṭṭa nilam
araiyum it

vaṟ celvatāka iṟaiyilicci kuṭutten
pirāntakan ciṟiyaveḷān nāṉa tirukkaṟṟaḷi piccanneṉ-

inscription.head:
No. 292.
(A.R. No. 292 of 1908.)
TIRUKKAḶITTAṬṬAI, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: North wall of the central shrine in the Vēdapurīśvara temple.

The record commences with the praśasti, Tirumaṉṉi-vaḷara-iru-nila-
maḍandaiyum, etc., of the king. It furnishes the details of date viz., year
9, Makara, Wednesday and Kēṭṭai (Jyēshṭha)-nakshatra.

It refers to a deed executed by the Sabhai of Vembārrūr alias
Śōḻamāttṁāṇḍa-chaturvēdimaṅgalam, a brahmadēya in Maṇṇi-nāḍu in
Rājēndraśiṅga-vaḷanāḍu to the Ādichaṇḍēsvaras, the mūlabhṛitya of the
temple of Śrīkuḍittiṭṭai-Paramasvāmi of the above village after receiving
a sum of 65 kāśu received from the latter, and granted remission of taxes
such as siddhāyam, paddy for pañchavāram, the taxes paid by the village
for cereals, oil, ghee, etc., ēri-īṟai, kulai, kurambu included in veṭṭi and vēdaṉai,
śilvari and others on the lands was made over to meet the requirements
of worship and food-offerings to the deity and the priest who performs
the worship. It is stipulated that the sabhai or the śrīkāryañ-cheyvār should
not collect the taxes or the bhōgam from the lands. The signatories who
attested the deed were learned brāhmaṇas-kramavits, sōmayājins and bhaṭṭas
residing in the quarters chēris of Vēmbaṟṟūr. The (chēris) referred to are
Arumoḻidēvachchēri, Jananāthachchēri, Nittavinōdachchēri, Rājakēsarichcheri,
Nigariliśōḻachchēri, Aḻagiyaśōḻachchēri, Śiṅgaḷāntakachchēri, Kundavaichchēri,
Śōḻakulasundarachchēri, Rājamāttāṇḍachchēri, Rājarājachchēri and
Śōḻamāttāṇḍachchēri.

inscription.head:
No. 292.
(A.R. No. 292 of 1908.)
TIRUKKAḶITTAṬṬAI, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: North wall of the central shrine in the Vēdapurīśvara temple.

The inscription begins with the praśasti, Tirumaṉṉi-vaḷara-iru-nila-
maḍandaiyum, etc., of the king. It furnishes the details of date viz., year
9, Dhanus, Sunday and Uttiraṭṭādi-nakshatra.

It records a deed executed by the Sabhai of Vembaṟṟūr alias
Śōḻamāttāṇḍa-chaturvēdimaṅgalam, a brahmadēya in Maṇṇi-nāḍu in
Vaḍagarai-Rājēndraśiṅga-vaḷanāḍu to the Chaṇḍēśvaradēva, the mūlabhṛitya
of the temple of Śrīkuḍittiṭṭai-Paramasvāmi of the village after receiving
85 kāśu received from the latter, and granted the remission of taxes on
the lands belonging to the above. The lands were to be enjoyed by the
temple as iṟaiyili and the bhōgam collected from it was made over to the
deity to meet the provision of food-offerings and to the priest who conducts
worship in the temple. The amount of 85 kāśu received from the
mūlabhṛityas of the temple include 60 kāśu of the share of the
śrībhaṇḍaram-śeyvār of Nāgadāma-kramavittan of Irāyūr and a share of
25 kāśu of the tiruparichāragam-śeyvārgaḷ Śrīrāma-kramavittaṉ, of the
temple of Rājarāja-Īśvaram-uḍaiyār.

The document was signed by the persons living in the different quarters
(chēris) of the village Vembaṟṟūr. It is stated that there was a kaḍigai
(ghaṭikā) in this village and the two madhyastas named Śrī Mādhavan-
kramavittan and Vīramēy-Iraṇḍāyiravaṉ who figure as signatories belong
to this kaḍigai. The suffix peruṅ-kāvidi ascribed to one of the madhyasthas,
named Kūttāḍi alias Tribuvanamagādēvī (Tribhuvana-mahādēvī) is interesting.

ṉṉavan vaitta cuntara muṭiyum intiraṉāramum teṇṭicaip

cūṭum kulataṉamākiya palar pukaḻ muṭiyum ceṅkatir mālaiyum

cciṉavil irupattorukāl araicukaḷai kaṭṭa paracurāmaṉ
meva

inscription.head:
No. 294.
(A.R. No. 294 of 1908.)
TIRUKKAḶITTAṬṬAI, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: North wall of the central shrine in the Vēdapurīśvara temple.

This record commences with the praśasti, Tirumaṉṉi-vaḷara-iru-nīla-
maḍandaiyum, etc., of the king.

It furnishes the details of date viz., regnal year 8, Kuṁbha, Thursday,
and Mūla-nakshatra.

It refers a deed executed by the mahāsabhai of Vembaṟṟūr alias
Śōḻamāttāṇḍach-chaturvēdimaṅgalam, a brahmadēya in Maṇṇi-nāḍu in
Rājēndraśiṅga-vaḷanāḍu to the Chaṇḍēśvaradēva, the mūlabhṛitya of the
temple of Śrīkuḍittittai-Paramasvāmi of the village. It states that the sabhai
received 50 kāśu from the latter in order to consecrate an image of Āḍavaḷār
(Naṭarāja) in the Sāttaṉār Naḍuvil-tirumuṟṟam at Kīḻaichchēri and granted
the remission of taxes like siddhāya, pañchavāram, ūriḍuvari, chēri-iḍuvāy
etc., The interest on the amount received and the taxes were agreed to be
paid by the sabhai. The bhōgam from out of these lands were made over
to the deity for the purpose of food-offerings and for the requirements
of the priest. The sabhai and the śrīkāryam-cheyvārgaḷ were not allowed
to collect taxes on these lands as the same was granted for the enjoyment
as adēyam. This order (ōlai) was recorded by Kaḍigai Madhyasthan
Nambipirān Śaṅkaranārāyaṇan alias Mūlapariḍai Brahmapriyan and attested
by a number of persons residing in the different quarters (chēris) of the
village Vēmbaṟṟūṟ. The names of different cheris are mentioned.

inscription.head:
No. 295.
(A.R. No. 295 of 1908.)
TIRUKKAḶITTAṬṬAI, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: West wall of the central shrine in the Vēdapurīśvara temple.

This record may palaeographically be assigned to c. 10th century. In
the absence of internal evidence to ascribe this epigraph to a different
Parakēsarivarman, it may be one of Parāntaka.

It records a gift of one-eighth śey (vēli) of land called Vēlipperuñ-śey
on the eastern bank of Sarasvati-vāykkāl to provide for food-offerings during
the ardhayāma service to the deity Śrīkuḍittiṭṭaipperumāḷ at Amaninārāyaṇach-
chaturvēdimaṅgalam, a dēvadāna and brahmadēya on the northern bank
of the river, by Ko[ṇḍ*]ampaṭṭi Dōṇaiya-kramavittan, a member of the
āḷuṅgaṇam of the above village.

The name of the village Amaninārāyaṇach-chaturvēdimaṅgalam
appears to have received the appellation after the time of Pallava King
Nandivarman III, who had the title Amaṉi (or Avaṉi) nārāyasa. Vēmbaṟṟūr
was so called in the time of Rājarāja I (See S.I.I., Vol. I, p. 325).
Tirukkaḷittaṭṭai, a hamlet of Vēmbaṟṟūr, on the northern bank of the river
(Kāvērī) was called Amaninārāyaṇach-chaturvēdimaṅgalam during the time
of Parakēsari rulers upto the period of Rājēndra I and thereafter from the
time of this king, assumed the name Śōḻamārttāṇḍa-chaturvēdimaṅgalam.
Perhaps, in the last years of Rājarāja I, it got the appellation Śōḻamārttāṇḍa-
chaturvēdimaṅgalam.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIX, No. 418.

inscription.head:
No. 296.
(A.R. No. 296 of 1908.)
TIRUKKAḶITTAṬṬAI, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p:
This inscription may palaeographically be assigned to the middle of
10th century A.D. The record may be assigned to Sundarachōḷa Parāntaka
II, whose other records from this place credit him with the title pāṇḍiyaṉaich-
chuṟam-iṟakkiṉa (who drove the Pāṇḍya into the forest).

It records a gift of one-eighth (of a vēli ?) of land to the west of the
tank, for maintaining a perpetual lamp in the temple of Tirukuḍittiṭṭaip-
perumāḷ at Amaninārāyaṇach-chaturvēdimaṅgalam, on the northern bank
of the river, by Porumāṉ Poṟṟi, wife of (maṇavāṭṭi) of a certain Neṟkuppai-
uḍaiyāṇ.
Published in S.I.I., Vol. XIX, No. 5.

inscription.head:
No. 297.
(A.R. No. 297 of 1908.)
TIRUKKAḶITTAṬṬAI, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: West wall of the central shrine in the Vēdapurīśvara temple.

This epigraph may be assigned to c. 10th century on palaeographical
grounds.

It registers a gift of a silver pot (kalaśam) for the sacred bath (abhishēka)
of god Śrīkuḍittiṭṭai-mahādēva of Vēmbaṟṟūr by Sembiyan-Mahādēvi-
pirāṭṭiyār, the mother of Uttamachōḻa (Uttamaśōḻadēvarai-tiru-vayiṟu-vāytta
Sembiyaṉ-Mahādēvip-pirāṭṭiyār). This silver vessel weighs 181 kaḻañju.

inscription.head:
No. 298.
(A.R. No. 298 of 1908.)
TIRUKKAḶITTAṬṬAI, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: West wall of the central shrine in the Vēdapurīśvara temple.
This record may palaeographically be assigned to c. 10th century.

The Rājarājakēsarivarman referred to here may be identified with
Rājarāja I.

It registers the grant of eṇmāv-arai-araikkāṇi lands made after purchase,
to provide for the supply of 108 pots of bathing water (snapanam-āḍi-
aruḷuvadaṟku) and for the food-offerings to be made on the day of makara-
saṅkrānti to the deity Tirukuḍittiṭṭai-mahādēvar of Avaninārāyaṇach-
chaturvēdimaṅgalam, a dēvadāna and brahmadēya on the northern bank
(of the river Kāvēri), by Kiḻānaḍigaḷ, daughter of Viḻupparaiyar and the
queen of Uttamachōḷa, son of Gaṇḍarādittadēva.

The other queen that is mentioned in an undated record from
Kāñchīpuram is Vīranāraṇiyār (See S.I.I., Vol. XXII, pt I, No. 3).

inscription.head:
No. 299.
(A.R. No. 299 of 1908.)
TIRUKKAḶITTAṬṬAI, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: South wall of the central shrine in the Vēdapurīśvara temple.

The record may be assigned to c. 10th century on palaeographical
grounds. This incomplete record, registers the gift of money in Iḻakkāśu
made by Kuñjiramalli, the wife and daughter respectively of Śiṟiyavēḷāṉ,
for burning lamps in the temple at Tirukkuḍittiṭṭai which was included
in Amaninārāyaṇa-chaturvēdimaṅgalam. Śiṟiyavēḷāṉ is identical with
Pirāntakaṉ Śiṟiyavēḷār, the general of the Chōḷa king Sundara-Chōḷa
Parāntaka II.

inscription.head:
No. 300.
(A.R. No. 300 of 1908.)
TIRUKKAḶITTAṬṬAI, KUMBHAKONAM TALUK, THANJAVUR DISTRICT.
inscription.p: South wall of the central shrine in the Vēdapurīśvara temple.

This incomplete inscription may palaeographically be assigned to c. 10th
century. This is probably assignable to Sundara Chōḷa.

It registers a gift of 25 īḻakkāśu for maintaining a perpetual lamp to
the deity Tirukuḍittiṭṭai-perumāḷ of Amaninārāyaṇa-chaturvēdimaṅgalam, a
dēvadāna and brahmadēya on the northern bank (of the river Kāvērī),
by Vimaladēvarasa Vāsudēvabhaṭṭa-Sōmāsi of Araṇaipuram. Also records
an endowment of some īḻakkāśu (figure lost) for a similar perpetual lamp
by Chandraśēkaraṉ Tirumoyaḍi ••• of Paṉaiyūr.
